records.ety: [L. dissipatus, p. p. of dissipare; dis- + an obsolete verb sipare, supare. to throw.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dissipated; p. pr. & vb. n. Dissipating.]

defs: To scatter completely; to disperse and cause to disappear; -- used esp. of the dispersion of things that can never again be collected or restored.
quotes: Dissipated those foggy mists of error.
The extreme tendency of civilization is to dissipate all intellectual energy.

defs: To destroy by wasteful extravagance or lavish use; to squander.
quotes: The vast wealth . . . was in three years dissipated.

defs: To separate into parts and disappear; to waste away; to scatter; to disperse; to vanish; as, a fog or cloud gradually dissipates before the rays or heat of the sun; the heat of a body dissipates.
To be extravagant, wasteful, or dissolute in the pursuit of pleasure; to engage in dissipation.
